name: Orestes
description: Orestes is a central figure in Greek mythology, primarily known for avenging the murder of his father, Agamemnon, by his mother, Clytemnestra, and her lover, Aegisthus. His story is one of familial betrayal, divine obligation, and the transition from primitive justice to a more civilized legal system. Born into the cursed House of Atreus, Orestes was sent away as a child to protect him from Clytemnestra's wrath after Agamemnon's return from the Trojan War. Years later, urged by the god Apollo, Orestes returns to Mycenae to fulfill his filial duty and avenge his father's death, a deed that plunges him into a cycle of guilt and madness, pursued by the Furies. His subsequent trial in Athens, presided over by Athena, marks a pivotal moment in Greek drama and signifies the shift from blood vengeance to a system of justice based on reason and law. Orestes' story explores themes of justice, revenge, fate, and the evolving nature of morality in ancient Greek society, showcasing the complexities of human action when faced with divine commands and familial obligations. His tale remains a powerful exploration of the burden of duty and the struggle for redemption.
subtitle: Avenger of Agamemnon, Pursued by Furies
achievements: Appealed to the Areopagus for justice
Established a precedent for legal trials

keyMyths.title: The Murder of Agamemnon
keyMyths.description: Agamemnon, upon returning from the Trojan War, is murdered by his wife Clytemnestra and her lover Aegisthus. This sets in motion the events that lead to Orestes' quest for vengeance and defines the initial conflict in the Orestes myth cycle.

keyMyths.title: The Vengeance of Orestes
keyMyths.description: Urged by Apollo, Orestes returns to Mycenae and, with the help of his sister Electra, kills Clytemnestra and Aegisthus. This act of matricide, while fulfilling his filial duty, brings upon him the wrath of the Furies.

keyMyths.title: The Trial at the Areopagus
keyMyths.description: Pursued by the Furies for his crime, Orestes seeks refuge in Athens, where he is tried before the Areopagus, a court established by Athena. The trial results in a hung jury, and Athena casts the deciding vote in favor of Orestes, acquitting him and ending the cycle of vengeance.

sources.author: Aeschylus
sources.description: A trilogy of plays including Agamemnon, The Libation Bearers, and The Eumenides, which detail the story of Orestes.
sources.text: A trilogy of plays including Agamemnon, The Libation Bearers, and The Eumenides, which detail the story of Orestes.

sources.author: Euripides
sources.description: A play focusing on Electra's role in the vengeance and her relationship with Orestes.
sources.text: A play focusing on Electra's role in the vengeance and her relationship with Orestes.

sources.author: Sophocles
sources.description: Another play focusing on Electra's role in the vengeance and her relationship with Orestes.
